content:
11 min read
Just now

--

A query that ran in four milliseconds on my laptop took eleven seconds in staging. Same code, same schema, same Postgres version. The only thing that had changed was the row count, and that turned out to be the entire story.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I have been letting coding agents write a large share of my backend for about a year now. Most of it is fine. Some of it is better than what I would have typed at two in the morning. But there is one specific thing they get wrong almost every single time, and it is the kind of wrong that stays invisible until the day your data finally gets big enough to punish you for it.
The mistake is not exotic. It is not a hallucinated table or a dropped WHERE clause that you would catch in review. It is quieter than that. The agent writes a schema that is technically correct, passes every test you throw at it, and ships clean. Then six months later somebody opens a support ticket about a page that takes ten seconds to load, and you go digging, and you find it.
The migration that looked perfect
